title: First Week With a New Puppy (2026) – Day-by-Day Survival Guide
excerpt: Your day-by-day guide to surviving the first week with a new puppy. Potty schedules, crate training, and sleep strategies that actually work.
quickAnswer: The first week with a new puppy focuses on three priorities: establishing a potty routine, introducing the crate as a safe space, and letting your puppy decompress. Keep visitors minimal, maintain a consistent schedule, and expect broken sleep for 3–5 nights.
whoThisIsFor: New puppy parents picking up their pup this week
Families wanting a structured first-week plan
Adopters bringing home a rescue puppy

sections.heading: Before Puppy Arrives: Home Prep
sections.content:
**Puppy-proof one room first** — don't try to secure your entire house. Pick a room with easy-clean floors (kitchen or laundry room) and block off the rest with baby gates. Remove shoes, charging cables, houseplants, and anything at puppy-mouth height.

**Set up the crate** — Place it in a common area where your puppy can see family activity but has a quiet corner. Add a washable bed or towel (expect accidents) and a safe chew toy. Cover three sides with a blanket to create a den feeling.

**Stock enzymatic cleaner** — You'll use more than you think. Buy at least two bottles. Regular cleaning products don't eliminate the scent markers that tell your puppy "this is a bathroom spot."

**Prepare the potty spot** — Choose one outdoor area and use it exclusively. Consistency helps your puppy associate that specific location with going potty. If you're apartment-living, set up an [indoor potty station](/collections/dog-potty-training) near the door.

**Buy high-value treats** — Soft, smelly, tiny. Your puppy needs to eat them instantly during training moments. Freeze-dried liver, cheese bits, or commercial training treats under 5 calories each.

sections.heading: Day 1–2: Decompression
sections.content:
**The biggest mistake new puppy parents make**: overwhelming their puppy with attention, visitors, and activities on day one.

Your puppy just left their mother, littermates, and everything familiar. They're stressed, confused, and exhausted.

**Day 1 priorities**:
- Drive home calmly (no loud music, minimal stops)
- Carry puppy to potty spot immediately upon arrival
- Let puppy explore the safe room at their own pace
- Offer water and a small meal
- Introduce the crate with door open, treats inside
- No visitors. No FaceTime introductions. Just calm.

**Day 2 priorities**:
- Begin potty schedule: out every 2 hours + after meals/naps/play
- Start feeding meals inside the crate (door open)
- Begin name recognition: say name → treat when puppy looks at you
- Allow naps in the crate with door open
- Continue limiting visitors

Expect crying at night. This is normal and not a sign that something is wrong. Your puppy is grieving the loss of their littermates. A warm water bottle wrapped in a towel and a ticking clock near the crate can help.

sections.heading: Day 3–5: Building Routine
sections.content:
By day 3, your puppy should start recognizing the daily rhythm. Now you can introduce structure.

**Sample daily schedule**:
- 6:30 AM — Potty outside immediately
- 6:45 AM — Breakfast in crate
- 7:00 AM — Supervised play (15 min)
- 7:15 AM — Potty
- 7:30 AM — Nap in crate (1–2 hours)
- 9:30 AM — Potty → play → training (5 min) → potty → nap
- Repeat cycle through the day
- 9:00 PM — Last potty, then bedtime in crate

**Start these habits now**:
- Hand-feeding some meals (builds food motivation for training)
- Handling exercises: gently touch paws, ears, and mouth daily
- Name game: 10 reps, 3 times daily
- Sit (lure with treat over nose — most puppies offer a sit within 5 tries)

Browse our [puppy essentials collection](/collections/puppy-essentials) for age-appropriate training tools and chew toys.

**What to ignore**:
- Whining in the crate (wait for 3 seconds of quiet before opening)
- Jumping (turn away, reward four-on-the-floor)
- Mouthing (redirect to an appropriate chew toy)

sections.heading: Day 6–7: First Steps Outside
sections.content:
If your puppy has had their first round of vaccinations, you can begin very limited outdoor exposure. Stay in clean, low-traffic areas — avoid dog parks, pet stores, and areas with unknown dog feces until your vet gives the all-clear.

**First leash walk**:
- Use a lightweight [harness and leash](/collections/dog-leash-control)
- Walk in your yard or a quiet sidewalk
- Let your puppy lead — this isn't exercise, it's sensory exploration
- Reward calm behavior near mild distractions (passing car, distant person)
- Keep it under 10 minutes

**Socialization (safe version)**:
- Carry your puppy to experience new sounds and sights
- Sit on a bench and let them observe the world
- Introduce one new surface (grass, concrete, gravel)
- Play recordings of thunderstorms and doorbells at low volume during meals

**End-of-week assessment**:
- ✅ Puppy goes to potty spot on their own?
- ✅ Puppy enters crate voluntarily for meals?
- ✅ Puppy responds to their name 50%+ of the time?
- ✅ Night crying reduced from day 1?

If you can check most of these, you're ahead of schedule. See our [complete first 30 days guide](/guides/puppy-training-first-30-days) for what comes next.

faq.question: Should I let my new puppy sleep in my bed?
faq.answer: Not during the first week. Crate training establishes independence and prevents separation anxiety. Once your puppy is potty trained and comfortable alone, you can transition to bed sleeping if desired.

faq.question: How many hours a day should a new puppy sleep?
faq.answer: Puppies sleep 18–20 hours per day. Enforce naps in the crate every 1–2 hours of awake time. An overtired puppy becomes nippy, hyper, and harder to train.

faq.question: When can I start taking my puppy outside?
faq.answer: Your puppy can go in your own yard immediately. Public areas with unknown dogs should wait until 1–2 weeks after the second vaccination round, typically around 12–14 weeks.

faq.question: Is it normal for my puppy to cry all night?
faq.answer: Yes, especially the first 2–3 nights. Your puppy is adjusting to being away from their littermates. A warm water bottle and a worn t-shirt with your scent can help. Most puppies settle by night 4–5.
